step2_pragmatics.implicature: Indu implies that Maya's response is disproportionate to the actual scale of the offence — 'एक या दो चीज़ें' quantifies the transgression as minimal, implicitly arguing that this quantity does not warrant the level of suffering Maya has performed; 'तो रखी' further implies 'and that's all I did — nothing more serious than that'; Indu is simultaneously defending himself and, entirely obliviously, invalidating Maya's emotional response by reducing it to a quantity dispute; he has exited apology mode and entered negotiation mode at precisely the wrong moment

rising intonation question mark on an obviously absurd claim performs sardonic invitation to self-incrimination

speaker: Indu
utterance: हाँ. भारी है न. तो मैं इसे dumbells की तरह use करता हूँ. you see?
context: Maya: exercise?

step1_metacognition.relationship: Husband (Indu) doubling down on his Kama Sutra exercise defence in response to Maya's devastatingly minimal 'exercise?' — rather than retreating, Indu has chosen to fully commit to and elaborate his implausible claim
step1_metacognition.cultural_knowledge_needed: Indu's decision to double down rather than retreat is the scene's greatest act of comic self-destruction — Maya's 'exercise?' was an invitation to reconsider, and Indu has instead chosen to construct an entire biomechanical justification; using the Kama Sutra as a dumbbell because it is heavy is a physically plausible claim (books can be heavy) that is simultaneously the most absurd possible response to Maya's incredulity; 'dumbells की तरह use करता हूँ' introduces English fitness vocabulary ('dumbbells', 'use') to support what is essentially a claim that he does bicep curls with an ancient erotic text; 'you see?' as a closing tag is a classic Indu move — seeking validation and comprehension-confirmation from the very person most likely to deny both; the 'हाँ' opener is particularly bold — he is affirming his prior claim before elaborating it, which suggests either genuine belief in the defence or panic-driven commitment
step1_metacognition.uncertainties: Whether Indu genuinely believes this defence or is constructing it desperately in real time cannot be resolved — both produce maximum comedy; 'you see?' directed at Maya is either obliviously optimistic or the most inadvertently comic question ever asked

step2_pragmatics.implicature: Indu implies that the Kama Sutra's physical weight is its relevant property for his purposes — weight makes it a dumbbell substitute, making the text's actual content irrelevant to his use case; he further implies through 'you see?' that this explanation is comprehensible and reasonable, inviting Maya's agreement; the underlying implicature of the entire utterance is that Indu would rather construct an increasingly elaborate absurd justification than acknowledge the obvious — a defence strategy that is simultaneously admirable in its commitment and catastrophic in its execution
step2_pragmatics.pragmatic_meaning: Indu commits fully to the exercise defence by providing a specific mechanism — the book's weight makes it a dumbbell substitute — and seals this with 'you see?' which performs a request for validation that Maya will almost certainly deny with maximum effect; the explanation is internally coherent (heavy book = dumbbell) while being externally absurd in context, and Indu's 'you see?' reveals his genuine hope that logical coherence will be sufficient to satisfy Maya, which is perhaps the most endearing and catastrophic misreading of the situation possible.

step3_verification.supporting_evidence: 'हाँ' opener — bold affirmation of prior claim before elaboration, committing fully to the defence; 'भारी है न' — physical property claim (it is heavy) establishing the dumbbell logic; 'tो' connecting weight to use — logical connective performing genuine reasoning; 'dumbells की तरह' — English fitness term embedded in Hindi syntax, Indu deploying modern fitness vocabulary to legitimise the claim; 'use करता हूँ' — present habitual establishing ongoing practice; 'you see?' — English validation-seeking tag directed at the least sympathetic possible audience, the scene's comic cherry on top
step3_verification.low_confidence_flags: Indu's sincerity vs panic-construction balance is unresolvable but both equally funny; whether he physically demonstrates the dumbbell motion during delivery would add an entire layer of physical comedy unrecoverable from text

sarcasm.label: not sarcastic
sarcasm.explanation: Indu's defence is completely sincere — he has committed to the exercise explanation and is now providing genuine supporting reasoning (weight → dumbbell use); there is no ironic gap between what he says and what he intends; 'you see?' is a sincere request for comprehension and agreement; the comedy is entirely situational and character-derived from the spectacular implausibility of a sincerely delivered claim.
linguistic_cues: 'हाँ' affirmation signals sincere commitment to the position not ironic acknowledgement
'भारी है न' physical reasoning is genuine supporting logic not ironic justification
'you see?' as sincere validation-seeking tag is the anti-sarcasm — it performs genuine hope for agreement

speaker: Maya
utterance: ये सारी चीज़ें मेरे cupboard में क्यों हैं?
context:
Maya: exercise?
Indu: हाँ. भारी है न. तो मैं इसे dumbells की तरह use करता हूँ. you see?

step1_metacognition.relationship: Wife (Maya) abandoning item-by-item cross-examination and returning to the original core charge — Maya has decided that the Kama Sutra-as-dumbbell defence is beneath engagement and pivots back to the fundamental territorial violation
step1_metacognition.cultural_knowledge_needed: Maya's pivot is a masterclass in prosecutorial strategy — having produced the Kama Sutra as her exhibit-A punchline and received Indu's dumbbell defence, she declines to engage with that absurdity any further and returns to the original, unanswered charge: why are your things in my cupboard at all; this is structurally equivalent to a lawyer who, after a witness's implausible answer, simply repeats the original question; 'ये सारी चीज़ें' (all these things) restores the cumulative framing — she is not asking about the Kama Sutra specifically but about the entire collection of items, which collapses Indu's 'एक या दो' defence retroactively; 'मेरे cupboard' — the possessive 'मेरे' combined with English 'cupboard' reasserts Maya's territorial sovereignty with maximum precision; the question is simple, direct, and unanswerable
step1_metacognition.uncertainties: Tone is critical — Maya's question could be delivered with cold fury, sardonic patience, or weary exasperation; all are consistent with her character but produce different comic textures; whether she is holding the Kama Sutra while asking this adds physical comedy unrecoverable from text

step2_pragmatics.implicature: Maya implies that no answer Indu has given — including the dumbbell defence — has addressed the actual violation, which is the presence of his things in her cupboard regardless of their individual justifications; she further implies that 'सारी चीज़ें' (all these things) constitutes a quantity significantly larger than 'एक या दो', silently demolishing that defence; the question is rhetorical in structure but demands a real answer — the 'क्यों' (why) is the simplest and most devastating interrogative Maya could deploy because it requires Indu to justify not one item but the entire pattern
step2_pragmatics.pragmatic_meaning: Maya abandons the item-specific cross-examination and returns to the structural charge with devastating simplicity — 'why are all these things in my cupboard?' is the question Indu cannot answer because the true answer (he has no other place, or he thought she wouldn't notice) is more damaging than any defence; by using 'सारी चीज़ें' she collapses the dumbbell defence, the 'एक या दो' minimisation, and the entire preceding negotiation into one unanswerable question; the pivot from theatrical outrage to cold simple interrogation is Maya's most powerful register move.

step3_verification.supporting_evidence: 'ये सारी चीज़ें' — 'all these things' restoring cumulative framing and demolishing 'एक या दो' retroactively; 'मेरे cupboard' — possessive assertion of territorial sovereignty, 'मेरे' in Hindi + English 'cupboard' combining both registers; 'क्यों' — single interrogative demanding justification for the entire pattern not just one item; absence of any reference to the Kama Sutra dumbbell defence — Maya has declined to engage with it entirely, which is itself the most contemptuous possible response; clean simple sentence structure after elaborate prior rhetoric — the simplicity performs controlled fury
step3_verification.low_confidence_flags: Delivery register — cold fury vs sardonic patience vs weary exasperation — prosody-dependent; whether physical props (holding items) accompany the question is unconfirmable

sarcasm.label: sarcastic
sarcasm.explanation: The question is sarcastic in structure because Maya already knows the answer — Indu put his things there, probably habitually, and she is not seeking information but performing a demand for acknowledgement of the violation; the gap between the question's literal form (genuine inquiry) and its actual function (prosecutorial assertion that no justification exists) is the sarcastic mechanism; additionally, by asking 'सारी चीज़ें' after the dumbbell defence, she is implicitly and sarcastically treating Indu's entire chain of justifications as having answered nothing
linguistic_cues: 'क्यों' as rhetorical interrogative performing demand not genuine inquiry
'सारी चीज़ें' sarcastic counter to 'एक या दो' — quantity challenge embedded in apparently neutral question
deliberate non-engagement with dumbbell defence is itself sarcastic dismissal through silence

speaker: Indu
utterance: क्योंकि मेरे cupboard में जगह ही नहीं है, Maya.
context: [Scene opening]

step1_metacognition.relationship: Husband (Indu) finally answering Maya's core question 'ये सारी चीज़ें मेरे cupboard में क्यों हैं?' — after all the deflections, minimisations, and the dumbbell defence, Indu has arrived at the actual truth
step1_metacognition.cultural_knowledge_needed: This utterance is the scene's emotional and comedic resolution from Indu's perspective — the honest answer that was always underneath the entire defensive architecture; 'मेरे cupboard में जगह ही नहीं है' (there is simply no space in my cupboard) is devastating in its plainness because it retroactively explains and humanises everything: the 'एक या दो चीज़ें', the Kama Sutra, the dumbbell defence — all of it was simply the consequence of a man who has no storage space in his own home; 'ही' as an emphatic particle performs the finality and obviousness of this fact — 'there just isn't any space' — as though this should have been self-evident all along; the first-name address 'Maya' softens the statement into something almost plaintive; the scene-opening tag suggests a scene cut to Indu finally delivering the real answer, which structurally positions this as the confession that ends the defensive cycle
step1_metacognition.uncertainties: Whether this is delivered with resignation, mild defiance, or plaintive honesty cannot be confirmed without audio; the scene-opening cut suggests this may be delivered after a pause or scene transition that gives it additional weight

step2_pragmatics.implicature: Indu implies that his use of Maya's cupboard was not malicious, not careless, and not a territorial violation — it was pragmatic necessity born of inadequate personal storage; he further implies, without stating it, that the real question is not why his things are in Maya's cupboard but why he doesn't have enough space in his own — which is itself a gentle indictment of the household's spatial politics; 'ही' implies 'this is simply the reality, what else was I supposed to do?'; the first-name 'Maya' performs a quiet appeal to her as a fellow reasonable person rather than as an authority figure
step2_pragmatics.pragmatic_meaning: Indu's honest answer dissolves the entire defensive scaffolding of the prior exchanges and replaces it with a simple domestic truth — he has no space; this is simultaneously the funniest possible explanation (all that theatre for a storage problem) and the most humanising moment for Indu in the entire scene; by addressing Maya by name and stating the fact plainly, he steps out of bumbling-defensive mode into something rare for his character: unguarded, direct honesty that makes him oddly sympathetic.

step3_verification.supporting_evidence: 'क्योंकि' — direct causal answer to Maya's 'क्यों', the first direct answer Indu has given since the cupboard dispute began; 'मेरे cupboard में' — 'मेरे' performing ownership claim that implicitly highlights the asymmetry with Maya's 'मेरे cupboard'; 'जगह ही नहीं है' — 'ही' emphatic particle performing 'simply, just, there just isn't' — finality and self-evidence; 'Maya' first-name address — intimate register, plaintive appeal; scene-opening placement suggests this is a confession delivered after a reflective pause; English 'cupboard' consistent with established scene vocabulary for this specific object
step3_verification.low_confidence_flags: Emotional register — resigned vs plaintive vs mildly defiant — prosody-dependent; whether Maya's response is sympathy, further contempt, or a practical solution shapes the scene's emotional landing

sarcasm.label: not sarcastic
sarcasm.explanation: This is the most genuinely sincere utterance Indu produces in the entire cupboard dispute sequence — it is the plain truth delivered without deflection, minimisation, or absurd justification; there is no ironic gap between what he says and what he means; the 'ही' emphatic performs honest emphasis not sarcastic stress; Indu has finally stopped defending and started telling the truth, and the utterance's sincerity is the source of its unexpected emotional weight.
linguistic_cues: 'क्योंकि' direct causal answer performing sincere explanation after defensive evasion
'ही' emphatic as sincerity marker — 'there simply is no space' is a statement of plain fact
first-name 'Maya' as intimate-plaintive address performing genuine appeal not defensive manoeuvre

speaker: Maya
utterance: अरे! तो जगह बनाओ न! यह दस–दस साल पुराने suit भर के रखा है तुमने. किसी ग़रीब भिखारी को क्यों नहीं देते हैं?
context: Indu: क्योंकि मेरे cupboard में जगह ही नहीं है, Maya.

step1_metacognition.relationship: Wife (Maya) responding to husband (Indu)'s honest admission of space shortage — Maya has received the real answer and immediately converts it into a practical directive while simultaneously delivering a class-inflected insult about Indu's wardrobe
step1_metacognition.cultural_knowledge_needed: Maya's response reveals her characteristic mode of engaging with domestic problems — she acknowledges the practical reality ('तो जगह बनाओ') but cannot resist embedding a class judgment within the solution; 'दस-दस साल पुराने suit' (ten-year-old suits) is a precise middle-class wardrobe insult — Maya's world is one of seasonal fashion and tailored elegance, and Indu holding onto decade-old suits is to her both a storage problem and a cultural failing; 'किसी ग़रीब भिखारी को क्यों नहीं देते हैं?' (why don't you give them to some poor beggar?) is the utterance's class-reveal moment — the suggestion is framed as charity but the specific choice of 'ग़रीब भिखारी' (poor beggar) rather than 'किसी ज़रूरतमंद को' (someone in need) or simply 'donate them' reveals Maya's contempt for Indu's suits — they are so outdated and middle-class that only a beggar would be an appropriate recipient; the 'अरे!' opener performs exasperated obviousness — 'of course the solution is to make space'
step1_metacognition.uncertainties: Whether Maya's charity suggestion is genuinely well-intentioned practical advice with an unfortunate class inflection, or deliberately insulting framing of Indu's wardrobe, cannot be fully resolved — both are simultaneously true to her character

step2_pragmatics.implicature: Maya implies (a) that the space problem is self-created and easily solvable, (b) that Indu's suits are so outdated they have no value to anyone except the destitute, (c) that Indu's attachment to old suits is both a practical inconvenience and a mark of his middle-class sentimentality about possessions; the 'न' in 'जगह बनाओ न' softens the directive minimally but the 'ग़रीब भिखारी' framing immediately undermines any charitable reading of her tone; she is simultaneously solving his problem and insulting his taste
step2_pragmatics.pragmatic_meaning: Maya pivots from the cupboard territorial dispute to a practical solution that is also a class-inflected attack — she tells Indu to declutter, identifies his decade-old suits as the culprit, and suggests donating them to beggars; the solution is functionally sound but the framing reveals Maya's contempt for Indu's wardrobe, his sentimentality about old clothes, and his middle-class aesthetic values; the entire utterance performs 'I am being practical and helpful' while actually being 'your taste is beneath contempt.'

step3_verification.supporting_evidence: 'अरे!' — obviousness/exasperation marker, performing 'the answer was always simple'; 'तो जगह बनाओ न' — practical directive with softening 'न', framing the solution as self-evident; 'दस-दस साल पुराने suit' — reduplication 'दस-दस' performing emphatic contempt for the age of the suits; 'भर के रखा है' — 'stuffed full' performing hoarding imagery; 'ग़रीब भिखारी' — class-reveal phrase, the specific choice of 'beggar' over neutral donation vocabulary; 'क्यों नहीं देते हैं' — rhetorical why-not performing both genuine suggestion and implicit judgment; pure Hindi throughout — Maya in practical domestic register without English affectations
step3_verification.low_confidence_flags: The exact proportion of genuine practical helpfulness vs deliberate class insult in Maya's intent cannot be confirmed — her character operates both simultaneously; whether Indu reacts to the practical advice or the 'भिखारी' insult determines the scene's next beat

sarcasm.label: sarcastic
sarcasm.explanation: The utterance is sarcastic in its framing of the solution — 'give them to a poor beggar' performs genuine charity advice while simultaneously encoding the sardonic subtext that Indu's suits are so worthless that beggars are their appropriate recipients; the gap between the surface speech act (practical decluttering advice) and the embedded meaning (your wardrobe is fit only for the destitute) is the sarcastic mechanism; 'दस-दस साल पुराने' as an emphatic insult further confirms the sardonic register
linguistic_cues: 'ग़रीब भिखारी' — specific choice of 'beggar' over neutral donation term is the sarcastic class-insult embedded in apparent practical advice
'दस-दस साल पुराने' — reduplication performing contemptuous emphasis on the suits' age
'अरे! तो जगह बनाओ न!' — exasperated obviousness performing 'you created this problem yourself' sarcastic framing

speaker: Indu
utterance: क्योंकि मैं उनका धंधा चौपट नहीं करना चाहता. I mean… ज़रा सोचो, Maya. अगर suit पहनके भिखारी भिक मांगें तो उन्हें भिक देगा कौन?
context:
Indu: क्योंकि मेरे cupboard में जगह ही नहीं है, Maya.
Maya: अरे! तो जगह बनाओ न! यह दस–दस साल पुराने suit भर के रखा है तुमने. किसी ग़रीब भिखारी को क्यों नहीं देते हैं?

step1_metacognition.relationship: Husband (Indu) counter-arguing Maya's suggestion to donate his suits to beggars — Indu has constructed an elaborate socioeconomic argument for why donating suits to beggars would harm their livelihood, delivered with complete earnestness
step1_metacognition.cultural_knowledge_needed: This is Indu's finest moment in the entire analysed corpus — his counter-argument is structurally sound within its own comic logic; beggars solicit alms partly on the basis of visible destitution, and a beggar wearing a suit would undermine the visual cues that generate charitable giving; Indu is deploying genuine economic reasoning ('धंधा चौपट नहीं करना चाहता' — don't want to ruin their business/livelihood) to defend keeping his old suits, which transforms a self-interested position into an apparently altruistic one; 'I mean…' as an English discourse marker performs the register of someone carefully formulating a considered argument, which is both characterologically surprising for Indu and comic because it precedes an argument about beggar economics; 'ज़रा सोचो, Maya' (just think about it, Maya) is the utterance's most audacious element — Indu is inviting Maya to reason with him as an equal, which reverses the usual power dynamic entirely; the argument is simultaneously absurd and internally coherent — classic Indu at his best
step1_metacognition.uncertainties: Whether Indu believes his own argument or has constructed it primarily to avoid donating his suits is genuinely ambiguous — both are equally funny and equally consistent with his character; the warm concern for the beggars' livelihood ('धंधा चौपट नहीं करना') may be sincere middle-class empathy or inspired self-justification

step2_pragmatics.implicature: Indu implies that Maya's solution, while apparently charitable, would have unintended negative economic consequences for the beggars themselves — he is performing concern for vulnerable people while simultaneously protecting his own attachment to his old suits; 'ज़रा सोचो' implies Maya has not thought this through, which is an extraordinary role-reversal from their usual dynamic; the rhetorical question 'उन्हें भिक देगा कौन?' (who would give them alms?) is the argument's logical conclusion and performs genuine rhetorical confidence; Indu is implicitly accusing Maya's casual 'give to beggars' suggestion of being thoughtless and potentially harmful
step2_pragmatics.pragmatic_meaning: Indu produces the scene's most sophisticated comic argument — an apparently altruistic economic defence of keeping his old suits; by framing suit-donation as a threat to beggar livelihoods and inviting Maya to 'just think about it', he simultaneously reverses the scene's power dynamic, transforms self-interest into apparent social concern, and delivers a logically structured argument that is internally coherent within its absurd premises; 'I mean…' and 'ज़रा सोचो, Maya' position him as the reasonable party, which is the scene's greatest character inversion.

step3_verification.supporting_evidence: 'क्योंकि' — direct causal answer performing genuine reasoning; 'धंधा चौपट नहीं करना चाहता' — 'don't want to ruin their livelihood/business', genuine economic concern framing; 'I mean…' — English discourse marker performing considered-argument register, unusual for Indu; 'ज़रा सोचो, Maya' — 'just think, Maya', extraordinary role-reversal moment where Indu invites Maya to reason; 'अगर suit पहनके भिखारी भिक मांगें' — conditional clause constructing the logical scenario; 'उन्हें भिक देगा कौन?' — rhetorical question as logical conclusion, performing genuine argumentative confidence; Maya first-name address repeated, maintaining the scene's intimate direct register
step3_verification.low_confidence_flags: Degree of self-awareness in Indu's argument — genuine altruistic concern vs inspired self-justification — cannot be resolved; both readings produce maximum comedy and maximum character richness

sarcasm.label: not sarcastic
sarcasm.explanation: Indu's argument is delivered with complete sincerity and genuine logical structure — he is not being ironic about the beggars, not mocking Maya's suggestion sarcastically, and not performing false concern; the argument is earnestly constructed and earnestly delivered; the comedy is entirely situational — a bumbling man producing a coherent socioeconomic argument to avoid donating his old suits — with no ironic gap between what Indu says and what he means.
linguistic_cues: 'क्योंकि' direct causal reasoning with no ironic framing
'ज़रा सोचो' sincere invitation to reason performed with genuine confidence
rhetorical question 'उन्हें भिक देगा कौन?' performing logical conclusion not sarcastic dismissal

speaker: Maya
utterance: तुम्हें उनकी ज़्यादा फ़िक्र है या मेरी? मुझे मेरा जवाब मिल गया, Indu. Thanks very much.
context:
Indu: क्योंकि मेरे cupboard में जगह ही नहीं है, Maya.
Maya: अरे! तो जगह बनाओ न! यह दस–दस साल पुराने suit भर के रखा है तुमने. किसी ग़रीब भिखारी को क्यों नहीं देते हैं?
Indu: क्योंकि मैं उनका धंधा चौपट नहीं करना चाहता. I mean… ज़रा सोचो, Maya. अगर suit पहनके भिखारी भिक मांगें तो उन्हें भिक देगा कौन?

step1_metacognition.relationship: Wife (Maya) delivering the scene's closing verdict on husband (Indu) — Maya has received Indu's beggar-economics argument and is now performing a wounded-dignity exit that reframes the entire dispute as a question of his priorities between her and strangers
step1_metacognition.cultural_knowledge_needed: Maya's rhetorical pivot is her most personally revealing move in the scene — by asking 'तुम्हें उनकी ज़्यादा फ़िक्र है या मेरी?' (do you care more about them or about me?) she transforms a storage dispute into a loyalty and affection question; this is classic Maya: she cannot acknowledge that Indu's argument was clever or that her suggestion had a flaw, so she reframes the entire exchange as evidence of emotional neglect; 'मुझे मेरा जवाब मिल गया' (I have received my answer) performs the devastating closure of someone who has confirmed their worst suspicions — the 'answer' she claims to have received is not about cupboard storage but about where she ranks in Indu's concern hierarchy; 'Thanks very much' is the scene's English-register exit line — performed with maximum sardonic frost, it is the British-inflected courtesy formula deployed as a weapon of dignified dismissal; 'Indu' by name at this moment performs a cold distance after the earlier intimate address patterns
step1_metacognition.uncertainties: Whether Maya is genuinely wounded by Indu's apparent prioritisation of beggars' welfare over her comfort, or is performing wounded dignity as a rhetorical move to win an argument she cannot win on logic, is deliberately ambiguous — and the comedy and pathos both operate simultaneously

step2_pragmatics.implicature: Maya implies that Indu's argument, however clever, revealed that he spent more cognitive and emotional energy protecting hypothetical beggars' livelihoods than considering her feelings about her cupboard space; 'मेरा जवाब मिल गया' implies the answer is damning — she ranks below beggars in Indu's hierarchy of concern; 'Thanks very much' implies she is done with the conversation and done with expecting Indu to prioritise her; the entire utterance implies that the cupboard dispute was never really about storage — it was about feeling valued, which is the scene's emotional revelation
step2_pragmatics.pragmatic_meaning: Maya delivers the scene's most emotionally complex exit — she reframes Indu's clever beggar-economics argument as evidence of her low priority in his affections, claims to have received a definitive answer to an unasked question about his care for her, and exits with 'Thanks very much' which performs sardonic British-register closure while encoding genuine hurt; the utterance is simultaneously Maya's most sardonic moment and her most vulnerable — the elaborate exit performance is itself evidence that she cares deeply about what it reveals.

step3_verification.supporting_evidence: 'तुम्हें उनकी ज़्यादा फ़िक्र है या मेरी?' — rhetorical comparison question reframing storage dispute as affection hierarchy; 'फ़िक्र' (concern/worry) — emotionally loaded word revealing the real stakes beneath the cupboard argument; 'मुझे मेरा जवाब मिल गया' — declarative closure performing confirmed suspicion, 'मेरा जवाब' implies a personal question that predates this exchange; 'Indu' — name address with cold distance after earlier intimate use; 'Thanks very much' — English exit formula deployed with maximum sardonic frost, register marker of performed British-style dignified dismissal; Hindi-English split: emotional content in Hindi, exit performance in English — consistent with Maya's register architecture
step3_verification.low_confidence_flags: The proportion of genuine hurt vs performed exit-for-effect cannot be resolved — both are simultaneously present in Maya's character; 'मेरा जवाब' implies a prior unasked question whose content is inferrable but not explicitly stated

sarcasm.label: sarcastic
sarcasm.explanation: 'Thanks very much' is the scene's most concentrated sarcasm — deployed as a closing courtesy in a context where Maya is furious and unthankful, it performs the maximum gap between literal meaning (genuine gratitude) and actual meaning (contemptuous dismissal); 'मुझे मेरा जवाब मिल गया' is also sarcastic in structure — she has received an 'answer' to a question that was never the real question, performing the sarcastic move of treating a storage argument as a relationship verdict; the entire exit performs wounded dignity so elaborately that it is simultaneously sincere and theatrical
linguistic_cues: 'Thanks very much' — British-register courtesy formula as sarcastic exit weapon, literal meaning entirely inverted
'मुझे मेरा जवाब मिल गया' — declarative of confirmed suspicion performing sarcastic closure on a question that was never asked aloud
rhetorical 'उनकी ज़्यादा फ़िक्र है या मेरी?' performing sarcastic reframing of beggars vs wife priority

step3_verification.low_confidence_flags: 'मिला' opener fragmentary; demolition speculation 'तुड़वा गए होंगे' slightly anomalous in context; who 'तुड़वाया' (caused demolition) unspecified; confidence moderated by these gaps

sarcasm.label: not sarcastic
sarcasm.explanation: Roshesh's revelation about the renovation closure is delivered with characteristic earnest sincerity — 'मामा मुझे भी लगा' performs genuine shared suspicion not ironic agreement, and 'तुड़वा गए होंगे' is sincere conspiratorial speculation; Roshesh's anti-ironic register is maintained consistently; the comedy of inadvertently undermining his own prior testimony is situational not linguistic.
linguistic_cues: 'रेनोवेशन के लिए बंद' reported as factual discovery not ironic commentary
'मामा मुझे भी लगा' as sincere shared-suspicion confirmation not sardonic agreement
'तुड़वा गए होंगे' as genuine conspiratorial speculation not ironic dismissal
